findings.title: SSRF via attacker-published on-chain DataLayer mirror URLs (unvalidated URL fetched by subscribed node)
findings.source: On-chain DataLayer mirror coin memo URLs. Any user can create a mirror coin: create_mirror_puzzle() = P2_PARENT.curry(1) (chia/wallet/db_wallet/db_wallet_puzzles.py:90) is a trivial puzzle anyone can spend into, and get_mirror_info (db_wallet_puzzles.py:97-110) extracts launcher_id and arbitrary url byte-strings from the CREATE_COIN memos with no validation. Mirror.decode_urls (chia/data_layer/data_layer_wallet.py:82) does url.decode('utf8') with no scheme/host checks.
findings.sink: aiohttp ClientSession.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename, proxy=proxy_url) in http_download() at chia/data_layer/download_data.py:313-318
findings.data_flow: DataLayer.periodically_manage_data() (data_layer.py:980) -> update_subscription() (data_layer.py:1076) -> update_subscriptions_from_wallet() (data_layer.py:953) calls wallet_rpc.dl_get_mirrors() and concatenates EVERY mirror's .urls into the subscription server list with only url.rstrip('/'). fetch_and_validate() (data_layer.py:607,628) iterates servers_info, calls insert_from_delta_file() -> download_file() (download_data.py:110) -> http_download() (download_data.py:298), which issues session.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename). The url is fully attacker-controlled (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data, http://127.0.0.1:8555/...). No scheme allowlist, host/IP filtering, or SSRF guard exists in this native path (urlparse/scheme checks appear only in the optional localhost-bound s3_plugin_service).
findings.exploitability: Attacker publishes a cheap mirror coin referencing the launcher_id of any store a victim node is subscribed to (subscriptions/owned stores are auto-tracked; launcher ids are public on-chain). On the next manage_data_interval cycle (default 60s) the victim node automatically issues attacker-directed outbound HTTP GETs to the chosen scheme/host/port plus '/<delta-filename>'. Blind SSRF: cloud instance metadata, internal admin/RPC ports, internal port scanning from the node. proxy_url defaults to None so requests go direct. Response body is size-capped and only written on success, so it is blind SSRF rather than full exfiltration -> High, not Critical.
findings.preconditions: Victim runs the data_layer service and is subscribed to (or owns) a store whose launcher_id the attacker targets; attacker can submit one transaction creating a mirror coin, no auth/relationship with victim required.

findings.title: Mirror URL list is unbounded and unfiltered, enabling forced outbound-request amplification
findings.source: On-chain mirror coin memos (same untrusted source as the primary finding): any user can publish mirror coins, each carrying multiple url byte-strings, for any subscribed launcher_id.
findings.sink: servers_info loop in fetch_and_validate() (data_layer.py:628) iterating every accumulated url and calling http_download per url/file.
findings.data_flow: update_subscriptions_from_wallet() (data_layer.py:953-959) does urls += mirror.urls for ALL mirror coins of a store with no cap on mirror/url count and no host de-duplication. These become ServerInfo entries fetched in fetch_and_validate (data_layer.py:624-686). Many mirror coins (or one with many url memos) make the victim attempt connections to each on every sync cycle, amplifying SSRF reach and outbound load.
findings.exploitability: Bounded by per-sync timeouts and the success-break (first working server stops iteration), and attacker pays per mirror coin. Mainly increases SSRF target surface and wasted outbound connections; reported as a contributing weakness, not standalone high severity.
findings.preconditions: Same as primary SSRF finding: victim subscribed to the targeted store; attacker can post mirror coins.

result.coverage_notes:
Reviewed the Wallet RPC cluster per the assignment: wallet_rpc_api.py (full, 3704 lines, route table + key/offer/notification/coin/sign handlers), supporting paths in notification_manager.py, trading/offer.py, util/puzzle_compression.py, and the transport/auth layer in rpc/rpc_server.py + server/server.py.

REACHABILITY GATE (decisive). Every endpoint in WalletRpcApi.get_routes() is served by the RpcServer over an aiohttp WebServer whose SSLContext is built by ssl_context_for_server (rpc_server.py:182), and server/server.py:71/100 sets ssl_context.verify_mode = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED. The RPC therefore requires a client certificate signed by the node's private CA (private_ca/ca generated at install). A remote or untrusted actor without that cert cannot invoke ANY wallet RPC endpoint, and the service binds to self_hostname (default localhost). Consequently the sensitive-but-by-design endpoints (/get_private_key returning the mnemonic via bytes_to_mnemonic at wallet_rpc_api.py:806; /log_in; /sign_message_by_address at 1862; /delete_all_keys; fund-moving /send_transaction, /take_offer, /send_notification) are gated behind cert-based mutual TLS and are NOT remote-unauth reachable. Reporting key disclosure / unsafe fund ops here would be a false positive against the stated threat model.

UNTRUSTED-INPUT PATHS examined (the genuinely attacker-influenced data, even if the RPC caller is authenticated):
- Offer parsing (counterparty-supplied): Offer.from_bech32 -> try_offer_decompression -> decompress_object_with_puzzles (puzzle_compression.py:73). Decompression bomb is mitigated: decompress_with_zdict caps output at 6*1024*1024 via zlib max_length (line 70), and version is bounds-checked (line 75). Subsequent parse goes through Rust WalletSpendBundle.from_bytes + CLVM validation. No unbounded alloc/CPU found.
- Peer notification ingestion: NotificationManager.potentially_add_new_notification (notification_manager.py:44) enforces required_notification_amount (default 1e8), de-dup, exact puzzle-hash match (construct_notification ... == coin_state.coin.puzzle_hash), and a 10KB message cap (line 69) before storing. Bounded; get_notifications returns stored, capped data.
- tx_endpoint wrapper (line 329) enforces sync status and rejects relative timelocks; TXConfig is autofilled from validated request fields.

LIMITATIONS: I did not dynamically verify the CA/cert provisioning, nor audit the daemon websocket (out of this cluster's scope) or the deeper Rust spend-bundle/CLVM validation (chia_rs, out of repo). If a deployment runs the wallet RPC with verify_mode relaxed or the CA key is shared, the key-disclosure/fund endpoints would become high severity — but that is a misconfiguration, not a code defect in this cluster. Honest result for the Wallet RPC cluster under the remote/untrusted threat model: zero defensible findings.

findings.title: SSRF: DataLayer node fetches attacker-controlled mirror URLs taken from unauthenticated on-chain coin spends
findings.source: On-chain CREATE_COIN memos of a mirror coin (P2_PARENT.curry(1) puzzle, no owner/signature check). Parsed in chia/wallet/db_wallet/db_wallet_puzzles.py:get_mirror_info() lines 107-109 (memos[1:] -> url list) and stored unvalidated by chia/data_layer/data_layer_wallet.py:coin_added() lines 780-795 via Mirror.decode_urls().
findings.sink: aiohttp ClientSession.get(server_info.url + "/" + filename, proxy=proxy_url) in http_download(), chia/data_layer/download_data.py lines 313-318.
findings.data_flow: Any party spends a coin on-chain creating a mirror coin (create_mirror_puzzle, anyone-can-create; amount can be minimal) whose CREATE_COIN memos encode launcher_id + arbitrary URL strings. When a victim DL node tracks that launcher_id (it tracks every store it subscribes to via DataLayer.subscribe -> dl_track_new, or that it owns), DataLayerWallet.coin_added() (data_layer_wallet.py:774-796) sees the mirror coin, calls get_mirror_info() to extract the URLs with zero validation, and stores them via dl_store.add_mirror(). The periodic DataLayer.periodically_manage_data() loop runs DataLayer.update_subscription() (data_layer.py:1076-1089) for every subscription, which calls update_subscriptions_from_wallet() (data_layer.py:953-959): it pulls all mirrors via wallet_rpc.dl_get_mirrors and writes their URLs straight into the subscriptions table (data_store.update_subscriptions_from_wallet, data_store.py:1645). It then calls fetch_and_validate() (data_layer.py:607-687) which reads those URLs via get_available_servers_for_store() and, for each, calls insert_from_delta_file() -> download_file() (download_data.py:110-145, with downloader None for plain HTTP) -> http_download() (download_data.py:298-318) issuing aiohttp GET to server_info.url + '/' + filename. No scheme allowlist (http/https only enforced implicitly by aiohttp), no private/link-local IP filtering, no DNS-rebinding protection, and aiohttp follows redirects by default. filename is constrained (bytes32 hex + fixed suffix) but the host/scheme/path-prefix of the URL are fully attacker-controlled.
findings.preconditions: Victim runs the data_layer service and is subscribed to (or owns) a store whose launcher_id the attacker targets. Attacker only needs the ability to create one mirror coin on-chain for that launcher_id (no auth, no relationship to the victim). The node must be in a state where fetch_and_validate attempts a download (i.e. local generation behind chain generation), which is the normal sync condition; even when synced the URL is persisted and used on the next lagging generation.
findings.exploitability: Remote, unauthenticated on-chain actor. Largely blind SSRF over HTTP(S): the node will issue GET requests to internal addresses (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/... cloud metadata, internal admin HTTP services, localhost-only ports), follow redirects, and the downloaded body is written to a size-capped file (max_delta_file_size, default 250 MiB) rather than returned to the attacker. Impact is internal-service request forgery / internal port reachability / metadata-endpoint probing and forced outbound connections from the node, plus a controllable target for failed-download bookkeeping. Not arbitrary file read (aiohttp does not honor file://).
findings.code_excerpt:
async with session.get(
    server_info.url + "/" + filename,
    headers=headers,
    timeout=timeout,
    proxy=proxy_url,
) as resp:

findings.title: SSRF: subscribe / add_mirror RPC accept arbitrary URLs that the node later fetches, with no scheme or host validation
findings.source: RPC request fields: data_layer_rpc_api.py subscribe() line 369 (request['urls']) and add_mirror() line 471 (request['urls']).
findings.sink: Same http_download() GET at chia/data_layer/download_data.py:313-318, reached via DataLayer.subscribe() (data_layer.py:869-876, only url.rstrip('/') applied) -> data_store.subscribe -> fetch_and_validate -> http_download.
findings.data_flow: DataLayerRpcApi.subscribe (data_layer_rpc_api.py:360-371) passes request['urls'] unchanged to DataLayer.subscribe (data_layer.py:869), which only does url.rstrip('/') and stores ServerInfo(url,0,0). The periodic manage loop then fetches each URL via http_download exactly as in finding 1. add_mirror (data_layer.py:939-944) likewise forwards arbitrary urls to the wallet to be published on-chain. No scheme/host/IP validation anywhere before the outbound GET.
findings.preconditions: Attacker can reach the DataLayer RPC endpoint (rpc_port). Normally bound to self_hostname with TLS client-cert mutual auth, so this requires the RPC to be exposed beyond localhost or the client cert/key to be reachable - a lower-likelihood precondition than the on-chain vector. Provided to document the missing input validation that makes finding 1 trivially weaponizable once any URL reaches the subscriptions table.
findings.exploitability: Same blind-HTTP SSRF primitive as finding 1 but requires RPC access (auth/localhost), hence Low for the remote-unauth threat model.
findings.code_excerpt:
async def subscribe(self, store_id: bytes32, urls: list[str]) -> Subscription:
    parsed_urls = [url.rstrip("/") for url in urls]
    subscription = Subscription(store_id, [ServerInfo(url, 0, 0) for url in parsed_urls])

findings.title: add_missing_files RPC writes DataLayer .dat files to a caller-supplied absolute directory (arbitrary-directory file write)
findings.source: RPC request field request['foldername'] in DataLayerRpcApi.add_missing_files (data_layer_rpc_api.py:413-414).
findings.sink: open(filename_full_tree/filename_diff_tree, 'xb'/'wb') inside write_files_for_root (chia/data_layer/download_data.py:93 and 101), via DataLayer.add_missing_files (data_layer.py:817-843) using server_files_location = foldername.
findings.data_flow: add_missing_files() converts the raw string to Path(request['foldername']) with no normalization or base-dir confinement and passes it as foldername to DataLayer.add_missing_files(), which uses it as server_files_location for get_full_tree_filename_path/get_delta_filename_path and then write_files_for_root() does filename_full_tree.parent.mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True) and open(...,'xb'/'wb'). An absolute path (e.g. a system directory the service user can write) causes directory creation and file writes outside the intended server_files location. File names themselves are constrained (store_id/node_hash hex), so it is directory-controlled writes of DAT content, not full arbitrary-path/arbitrary-content overwrite.
findings.preconditions: Attacker can call the DataLayer RPC (rpc_port). Same auth caveat as finding 2 (normally cert-authed/localhost). Service-user write permissions on the chosen directory.
findings.exploitability: Limited: requires RPC access and the written file names are fixed-format; impact is creating attacker-chosen directories and dropping .dat blobs there (potential disk-fill / writing into unexpected locations). Reported for completeness of the file-write surface.

result.coverage_notes:
Scope covered in full: data_layer_server.py (HTTP file/folder handlers), download_data.py (is_filename_valid, http_download, download_file, insert_from_delta_file, write_files_for_root), data_layer.py (subscribe/mirror/fetch_and_validate/upload flows), data_layer_rpc_api.py (all routes), data_store.py (read_from_file, insert_into_data_store_from_file, subscription/server-info storage, migrate_db), s3_plugin_service.py (download/upload/add_missing_files handlers), data_layer_wallet.py (coin_added/get_mirror_info/verify_offer), dl_wallet_store.py, data_layer_util.py (filename helpers), start_data_layer.py.

Things checked and judged NOT vulnerable / out of reach:
- Path traversal on the public DataLayer HTTP server (/{filename}, /{tree_id}/{filename}): well-defended. is_filename_valid (download_data.py:28-59) requires store_id/node_hash to be valid hex bytes32, generation an int, exact version suffix, and crucially reconstructs the canonical filename and compares for equality, so '..' / slashes / encoded traversal cannot pass. This is the natural place an auditor would expect a bug and it is correctly mitigated.
- s3_plugin_service download/upload: filename strictly validated via is_filename_valid, store_id cross-checked against filename prefix, and download gated by a config-driven url allowlist with scheme=='s3'; target paths confined to server_files_path. Also the plugin binds host='localhost'. Low remote relevance.
- read_from_file/insert_into_data_store_from_file: total file size capped (max_delta_file_size, default 250 MiB) before parse; per-node length reads are bounded by the file, so no clear unbounded-allocation DoS from the 4-byte length prefix. Hash integrity is enforced by the merkle DeltaReader (missing-hash resolution) so a malicious delta file cannot silently inject arbitrary tree state without matching root hashes.

The strongest issue is the on-chain mirror-URL SSRF (finding 1): it is the only one reachable by a fully untrusted remote actor (on-chain coin spend, no auth, no relationship to the victim) given the realistic precondition that the victim is subscribed to a store. Confidence is moderated (0.58) because (a) it requires the victim to be subscribed to/own the targeted store, and (b) it is HTTP(S)-only blind SSRF (aiohttp rejects file://, response bodies are not returned to the attacker), so impact is internal-request-forgery / metadata-probing rather than arbitrary file read or direct data exfiltration. I did not attempt dynamic verification (no running node / on-chain environment available); findings are from static data-flow analysis.

findings.title: DataLayer client SSRF via attacker-controlled on-chain mirror URLs (no scheme/host allowlist)
findings.source: On-chain DataLayer Mirror coin `urls` field. Anyone can create a Mirror coin for any launcher_id/store_id on-chain; it is returned by wallet_rpc.dl_get_mirrors and is fully attacker-controlled (untrusted, no auth).
findings.sink: aiohttp ClientSession.get(server_info.url + "/" + filename, proxy=proxy_url) in http_download() at download_data.py:313-318
findings.data_flow: DataLayer.update_subscriptions_from_wallet (data_layer.py:953) -> dl_get_mirrors returns Mirror.urls (on-chain, attacker-controlled) -> data_store.update_subscriptions_from_wallet stores them as ServerInfo.url -> DataLayer.fetch_and_validate (data_layer.py:607) reads get_available_servers_for_store and loops server_info -> insert_from_delta_file (download_data.py:171) -> download_file (download_data.py:110) with downloader=None -> http_download (download_data.py:298) -> session.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename). No validation that the URL is http(s) only, no host/IP allowlist, no block of RFC1918/localhost/link-local/metadata (169.254.169.254) targets. The fetch is a blind GET issued from the victim node.
findings.preconditions: Victim runs the data_layer service (chia_data_layer) and is subscribed to at least one store_id (operator opt-in via the data_layer RPC 'subscribe', or owns/tracks a store). Once subscribed, mirror URLs are pulled automatically from chain on the periodic update, so the attacker needs no further victim interaction: they add a Mirror coin for that store_id with a urls entry pointing at an internal target. proxy_url defaults to None.
findings.exploitability: Untrusted, remote, no-auth on-chain actor controls the destination of HTTP GET requests issued by the victim's DataLayer node. Enables blind SSRF: probing internal services, hitting cloud metadata endpoints, forcing connections to internal hosts. Limitations that cap severity: it is blind (response body is not reflected; it is parsed as a delta file and validated against expected root hashes before persistence), response size is bounded by max_delta_file_size, the path suffix is a fixed delta filename, and exploitation requires the optional data_layer service plus an existing subscription.
findings.code_excerpt: async with session.get(server_info.url + "/" + filename, headers=headers, timeout=timeout, proxy=proxy_url) as resp:

findings.title: RPC HTTP error handler returns full server-side traceback to the client
findings.source: Any exception raised inside an RPC endpoint handler while processing request_data = await request.json() (util.py:79) from an RPC client.
findings.sink: res_object = {..., "traceback": tb, ...} returned via obj_to_response (util.py:90-97), where tb = traceback.format_exc().
findings.data_flow: wrap_http_handler.inner (util.py:78) -> await f(request_data) raises -> except Exception (util.py:86) -> tb = traceback.format_exc() -> placed verbatim into the JSON HTTP response body under key 'traceback' and serialized to the client. This wrapper is applied to every RPC route via RpcServer.start (rpc_server.py:203: wrap_http_handler(func, route)).
findings.preconditions: Caller can reach an RPC port (full_node/wallet/etc.) and complete the mutual-TLS handshake (requires a cert signed by the per-install private CA). By default the RPC binds to self_hostname=localhost and enforces ssl.CERT_REQUIRED against the private CA (chia/server/server.py:67-72), so a pure-remote unauthenticated attacker cannot reach it. Reachable by local processes / any holder of a private-CA-signed cert, or if an operator rebinds the RPC to a non-localhost interface.
findings.exploitability: Low. The traceback can leak absolute filesystem paths, library versions, and internal state, aiding further attacks. Because the RPC is gated by mutual TLS bound to localhost in the default configuration, this is not remotely exploitable by an unauthenticated actor; severity is limited to information disclosure to an already-authorized/local client. The parallel websocket path (rpc_server.py safe_handle) does NOT include the raw traceback, only this HTTP wrapper does.

result.coverage_notes:
Scope covered in depth: chia/rpc/rpc_server.py, chia/rpc/util.py, chia/daemon/server.py, chia/daemon/keychain_server.py (all read in full), plus chia/server/server.py (SSL context), chia/server/ssl_context.py, chia/ssl/create_ssl.py, chia/util/network.py, chia/util/service_groups.py, chia/util/initial-config.yaml (binding defaults), the data_layer HTTP server + client (data_layer_server.py, download_data.py, data_layer.py), and the CHIP-0029 deserialization path (chia/rpc/util.py marshal + chia/wallet/util/clvm_streamable.py). A surface pass over full_node_api.py P2P handlers was also done.

NEGATIVE RESULTS (classes explicitly hunted, found NOT exploitable):

1. Daemon websocket CSWSH / missing auth (the headline concern): MITIGATED. The daemon WebSocketServer (daemon/server.py) and all RPC servers use ssl_context_for_server (server/server.py:55-72) with verify_mode = ssl.CERT_REQUIRED and cafile = root_path/private_ssl_ca/crt, i.e. MUTUAL TLS against a per-install RANDOM private CA (create_ssl.py make_ca_cert generates a fresh 2048-bit RSA CA per install). A browser cannot present a client cert, so classic cross-site websocket hijacking from a web page is blocked, and a non-localhost network attacker without the private CA key cannot complete the TLS handshake. There is NO origin-header check, but it is moot because of mTLS. The daemon also binds to self_hostname=localhost (daemon_port 55400) by default. The shipped chia_ca.crt/key is the PUBLIC network CA used only for P2P (public_ssl_paths) and is NOT the CA the daemon/RPC trust, so possessing the shipped CA does not grant daemon access.

2. Keychain access bypass over the daemon: keychain_commands (get_all_private_keys returns secret entropy, etc.) are reachable over the daemon ws and gate on is_keyring_locked but NOT on caller authorization; however reaching them still requires the mutual-TLS client cert above, so this is not a remote/low-priv bypass. The daemon get_keys_for_plotting / get_wallet_addresses (server.py:661-722) call Keychain().get_keys(include_secrets=True) WITHOUT a keyring-lock check, but they output only PUBLIC material (addresses, farmer/pool public keys), so no secret disclosure.

3. Command/argument injection via start_service / start_plotting (daemon/server.py:1269, 1158; launch_service:1456; launch_plotter:1410): all subprocess.Popen calls use shell=False with an argv list. start_service validates the service name via validate_service (service_groups.py:47), which is exact list membership (service in [list...]), so no arg smuggling. Plotter args are built as discrete argv tokens. Reaching these also requires the mTLS client cert. Not a remote/low-priv injection.

4. data_layer HTTP file server path traversal (data_layer_server.py file_handler/folder_handler, bound to 0.0.0.0 + UPnP, plain HTTP, no auth, genuinely internet-reachable): DEFENDED by is_filename_valid (download_data.py:28). It splits on '-', parses the first two tokens as bytes32 hex, requires version 'v1.0.dat' and type in {delta,full}, then reconstructs the canonical filename and requires reformatted == filename. Any '/', '..', or non-canonical input fails the equality check, so joinpath cannot escape server_dir. No bypass found.

5. P2P full-node DoS (request_blocks etc.): bounded by constants.MAX_BLOCK_COUNT_PER_REQUESTS (full_node_api.py:421-481) and the codebase's pervasive rate limiter; no obvious unbounded alloc/loop found in the time-boxed pass.

The two reported findings are the only issues with a plausible untrusted/low-privilege reach, and both carry honest caveats (SSRF is blind + subscription-gated; traceback disclosure is mTLS/localhost-gated). Zero critical/high remote-unauth findings in this cluster; the daemon/RPC core is well hardened.

findings.title: CPU DoS: untrusted counterparty offer runs attacker-controlled CLVM at near-block cost during offer summary/take
findings.source: Offer string supplied by an untrusted counterparty (e.g. pasted/imported into the wallet) -> wallet_rpc_api.get_offer_summary(GetOfferSummary.parsed_offer) / take_offer(TakeOffer.parsed_offer) / check_offer_validity
findings.sink: Offer._get_offered_coins -> inner_puzzle.run_with_cost(cost_left, inner_solution) with cost_left initialized to INFINITE_COST (11_000_000_000); also Offer.get_cancellation_coins line 430 run_with_cost(spend.puzzle_reveal, INFINITE_COST, spend.solution)
findings.data_flow: GetOfferSummary.parsed_offer (wallet_request_types.py:738 Offer.from_bech32) -> WalletRpcApi.get_offer_summary (wallet_rpc_api.py:2090) -> Offer.summary() (offer.py:339) -> get_offered_amounts()/get_requested_amounts() -> get_offered_coins() -> _get_offered_coins() (offer.py:238) where cost_left=INFINITE_COST (offer.py:241) and each offered parent's inner puzzle is executed via run_with_cost(cost_left, inner_solution) (offer.py:257). An attacker crafts an offer whose inner puzzle consumes up to the full ~11e9 cost budget, making a single summary/validity/take call burn roughly a max-cost-block's worth of CPU. take_offer (wallet_rpc_api.py:2156 -> trade_manager.respond_to_offer:812) reaches the same get_offered_coins path via arbitrage()/check_offer_validity.
findings.preconditions: Victim inspects or takes an attacker-supplied offer. The summary/CLVM cost is bounded by INFINITE_COST=11e9 (one block's worth) and decompression is capped at 6MB (puzzle_compression.decompress_with_zdict), so impact is bounded; meaningful disruption requires the victim to repeatedly process malicious offers. RPC itself is localhost-bound + mutual-TLS by default, so the caller is the wallet owner; the untrusted element is the offer content, not the caller.
findings.exploitability: Low. No memory blowup (6MB decompression cap) and CPU is bounded to ~1 max-cost block per call (the shared cost_left decrements across spends). Not amplifiable beyond the per-call budget in _get_offered_coins. Realistic only as repeated/automated offer inspection causing transient CPU spikes; not a clean single-shot remote crash.
findings.code_excerpt:
cost_left = INFINITE_COST  # offer.py:241
...
puzzle_cost, conditions = inner_puzzle.run_with_cost(cost_left, inner_solution)  # offer.py:257

findings.title: Full Python traceback returned in HTTP RPC error responses (info disclosure)
findings.source: Any RPC request that triggers an unhandled exception in a handler (e.g. malformed offer bech32, bad puzzle hash, type errors) submitted by an RPC client
findings.sink: wrap_http_handler.inner builds the JSON error response including "traceback": tb (full traceback.format_exc()) and returns it to the client via obj_to_response
findings.data_flow: WebServer route -> wrap_http_handler.inner (rpc/util.py:78) -> handler raises -> except branch (rpc/util.py:86) -> tb = traceback.format_exc() (rpc/util.py:87) -> res_object["traceback"] = tb (rpc/util.py:93) -> obj_to_response(res_object) sent to caller. The same verbose traceback is also written to logs (log.warning, rpc/util.py:88).
findings.preconditions: Caller can reach the RPC endpoint. The wallet RPC is localhost-bound and mutual-TLS gated by default (ssl.CERT_REQUIRED with the node's private CA, self_hostname=localhost), so a remote unauthenticated attacker cannot reach it unless the operator has rebound the RPC to a non-loopback interface and exposed the client cert/key. Disclosed data is internal file paths / code structure, not keys.
findings.exploitability: Very low as a standalone issue given the mTLS+localhost gate; provides minor internal-path/version reconnaissance to an already-authenticated RPC client. Included for completeness, not as an actionable remote bug.

result.coverage_notes:
Scope reviewed: chia/wallet/wallet_rpc_api.py (full route table + key-management, offer, NFT, DID, DL, VC, signing endpoints), wallet_state_manager.py (supporting), wallet_node_api.py (@metadata.request P2P handlers), trade_manager.py (respond_to_offer / offer flow), trading/offer.py (offer parse/summary/to_valid_spend), util/puzzle_compression.py, notification_manager.py, rpc/rpc_server.py, rpc/util.py (marshal + HTTP wrapper), server/server.py (TLS), and initial-config.yaml defaults.

KEY REACHABILITY FINDING (negative): The wallet RPC is bound to self_hostname=localhost and enforces MUTUAL TLS (ssl_context_for_server sets verify_mode=ssl.CERT_REQUIRED with the node's private SSL CA; server.py:55-72). A remote/unauthenticated attacker therefore CANNOT reach wallet RPC endpoints by default without the node's client cert+key. This caps the severity of every RPC-side issue. As a result I did NOT report several superficially-alarming endpoints as remote vulns:
 - get_private_key (wallet_rpc_api.py:803) returns the master secret key AND mnemonic seed; add_key, delete_key, delete_all_keys, log_in -- all by-design and mTLS-gated. No remote/low-priv reachability; not reported.
 - No mnemonic/private-key material is written to logs (verified via grep over chia/wallet); the error-path logs (799/840/852/949) only carry exception strings.

NEGATIVE RESULTS (checked, not vulnerable):
 - Parameter injection / path traversal: all RPC params are strongly typed via Streamable.from_json_dict; fingerprints are uint32; the only filesystem ops (delete_key/delete_all_keys path.unlink, wallet_rpc_api.py:854-860/952-958) derive the path from get_wallet_db_path using str(uint32 fingerprint) -> no attacker-controlled path. No open()/write of attacker strings in the wallet RPC.
 - Decompression bomb on offers: decompress_with_zdict caps output at max_length=6*1024*1024 (puzzle_compression.py:70); offer body bounded by RPC max_request_body_size (default 1MB; config rpc_server_max_request_body_size 25MB). Bounded; not reported.
 - Offer fund-theft (summary vs executed-spend mismatch): summary() and to_valid_spend() both derive from the same _bundle/requested_payments, and respond_to_offer re-derives arbitrage() from the actual spend and enforces is_valid() (arbitrage>=0). No mismatch primitive found in the time budget; this is a deep, well-audited area -- absence of a finding is not proof of safety.
 - Notifications: incoming on-chain notification message capped at 10KB and gated by required_notification_amount (notification_manager.py:69); returned hex-encoded in JSON so no injection.
 - P2P wallet_node_api.py handlers are mostly client-side response handlers feeding new_peak_queue; the untrusted coin_state_update/new_peak_wallet path leads into wallet_state_manager validation (out of this cluster's depth budget and not obviously exploitable from a quick read).

Minor note (not a standalone finding): Offer.from_bech32 calls bech32_decode(offer_bech32, max_length=len(offer_bech32)) (offer.py:693), passing the input's own length as the max -- neutralizing the bech32 length guard. Bounded in practice by the HTTP body-size limit, so no independent impact.

Honest bottom line: No high/critical remotely-reachable, low-privilege vulnerability was confirmed in the Wallet RPC cluster. The two items reported are Low/Info, both fundamentally constrained by the localhost+mTLS posture and by existing cost/size caps. Zero high-severity findings is the honest answer for this cluster under the stated threat model.

result.exploit_sketch:
Blind SSRF via unauthenticated on-chain mirror coins. The DataLayer mirror puzzle create_mirror_puzzle() = P2_PARENT.curry(1) (chia/wallet/db_wallet/db_wallet_puzzles.py:90-94) is anyone-can-create with no owner key or signature, by design (mirrors are permissionless host announcements).

Concrete exploit:
1. Victim runs a chia data_layer service and has subscribed to some store launcher_id L (the normal DL use case). subscribe() -> wallet_rpc.dl_track_new() inserts L into singleton_records, so is_launcher_tracked(L) returns True (dl_wallet_store.py:293-302).
2. Attacker spends ANY coin they control on-chain, producing a CREATE_COIN to MIRROR_PUZZLE_HASH with a small amount (e.g. 1 mojo). The spend's CREATE_COIN memo list is [L, b"http://169.254.169.254:80", ...] -- launcher_id followed by arbitrary URL byte strings. No signature tying the spend to L's owner is required.
3. The victim wallet observes the new mirror coin. DataLayerWallet.coin_added() (data_layer_wallet.py:773-796) fetches the parent spend, runs get_mirror_info() (db_wallet_puzzles.py:97-110) which returns (L, memos[1:]) with zero validation, checks only is_launcher_tracked(L)==True, and stores Mirror.decode_urls(urls) (plain utf-8 decode, no checks) via dl_store.add_mirror().
4. On the next periodically_manage_data() cycle (default 60s), update_subscription() -> update_subscriptions_from_wallet() (data_layer.py:953-959) pulls the attacker URLs via dl_get_mirrors and writes them straight into the subscriptions table.
5. fetch_and_validate() (data_layer.py:607+) reads them through get_available_servers_for_store() and calls insert_from_delta_file() -> download_file() (downloader=None for plain http, so http_download) -> http_download() (download_data.py:298-318) issues aiohttp session.get(server_info.url + "/" + filename). filename is the fixed-format "{store_id}-{node_hash}-delta-{gen}-v1.0.dat"; the scheme/host/port/path-prefix of server_info.url are fully attacker-controlled.

No scheme allowlist, no private/link-local/loopback IP filtering, no DNS-rebinding protection exist anywhere in the path (grep for urlparse/is_private/169.254/scheme over download_data.py + data_layer.py + data_store.py returns nothing). aiohttp follows redirects by default (allow_redirects not set), so an attacker URL can 30x-redirect to any internal target, further widening reach.

Impact = blind/semi-blind SSRF: the node can be coerced into GET requests to arbitrary internal/cloud-metadata endpoints (e.g. http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data path-prefix attacker-set, full path reachable via redirect). Response bodies are NOT reflected to the attacker, and downloaded delta-file content is later hash-validated against on-chain root hashes, so no data can be injected into the store. max_delta_file_size caps download size. So the value is internal port-scanning / hitting internal HTTP services / metadata exfil only if the target service leaks via timing/error behavior, not direct data return.

Limiting factors keeping this at Medium rather than High: (a) requires the victim to be subscribed to / own a store launcher_id (is_launcher_tracked gate) -- though that is the routine DataLayer state and the attacker can self-publish a store to lure subscribers; (b) blind (no response reflection); (c) path suffix is constrained to the .dat filename format absent a redirect.
result.reason: Survives against the real code as a genuine blind SSRF. Source is unauthenticated and fully attacker-controlled: create_mirror_puzzle() = P2_PARENT.curry(1) is anyone-can-create with no signature, and get_mirror_info() (db_wallet_puzzles.py:107-109) + Mirror.decode_urls() (data_layer_wallet.py:82-83) extract memo URLs with zero validation. coin_added() (data_layer_wallet.py:773-796) stores them gated only by is_launcher_tracked(launcher_id). The flow to the sink is intact: periodically_manage_data -> update_subscription -> update_subscriptions_from_wallet (data_layer.py:953-959) -> fetch_and_validate (data_layer.py:607+) -> insert_from_delta_file -> download_file (downloader None) -> http_download (download_data.py:313-318) issuing aiohttp GET to server_info.url. I confirmed NO scheme allowlist, NO private/link-local IP filtering, and NO DNS-rebinding protection anywhere in the download path; aiohttp follows redirects by default. The host/scheme/port are attacker-controlled; only the .dat path suffix is fixed. Severity stays Medium (not higher) because it is blind: response bodies are never returned to the attacker, downloaded content is hash-validated against on-chain roots so no store-data injection is possible, max_delta_file_size caps the fetch, and exploitation requires the victim to be tracking the launcher_id (the normal subscribed state, and the attacker can self-publish a luring store, so this is a soft gate). The candidate's data_flow, source/sink line references, and 'no validation' claims all check out against the real chia code.

findings.title: DataLayer offer summary raises unhandled StopIteration on crafted graftroot offer (per-request DoS)
findings.source: Attacker-supplied offer string -> WalletRpcApi.get_offer_summary(request) with advanced=False (chia/wallet/wallet_rpc_api.py:2090-2095), parsed via GetOfferSummary.parsed_offer = Offer.from_bech32 (chia/wallet/wallet_request_types.py:737-739)
findings.sink: next(cs for cs in offer.coin_spends() if cs.coin.parent_coin_info == spend.coin.name()) in DataLayerWallet.get_offer_summary
findings.data_flow: get_offer_summary RPC -> TradeManager.get_dl_offer_summary(offer) (trade_manager.py:968-980) -> DataLayerWallet.get_offer_summary(offer) -> iterates offer.coin_spends(); for a spend whose puzzle matches a DL singleton and whose graftroot module == GRAFTROOT_DL_OFFERS, it calls next(...) to find a child spend (parent_coin_info == spend.coin.name()). An attacker crafts an offer containing a DL-singleton graftroot spend with NO matching child spend, so the generator is empty and next() raises StopIteration, which is not caught in get_offer_summary or get_dl_offer_summary. The RPC layer catches it (rpc_server.py:404) and returns an error, so impact is a single failed request, not a crash.
findings.exploitability: Low. Requires reaching the wallet RPC, which is gated by mutual TLS with the node's private CA (rpc_server.py:182). A caller who already holds a valid client cert (e.g., a GUI or co-located tool) or any path that feeds an untrusted offer into get_dl_offer_summary triggers an unhandled exception. The handler exception is caught by safe_handle/wrap_http_handler, so this is a noisy 500/error per request rather than a process crash. No amplification beyond one request.
findings.preconditions: Access to the wallet RPC get_offer_summary endpoint (mTLS client cert) OR another code path that passes a counterparty-supplied offer to DataLayerWallet.get_offer_summary. advanced must be False.
findings.code_excerpt: child_spend: CoinSpend = next(cs for cs in offer.coin_spends() if cs.coin.parent_coin_info == spend.coin.name())

findings.title: Offer summary offered/requested amounts are computed by an admitted heuristic, enabling UI deception of an offer taker
findings.source: Counterparty-supplied offer file/string taken by a victim (Offer.from_bech32 / from_bytes), summarized via get_offer_summary RPC or TradeRecord.to_json_dict_convenience
findings.sink: _get_offered_coins() amount-matching heuristic feeding get_offered_amounts() and summary()
findings.data_flow: A victim receives an offer and the wallet/GUI summarizes it: get_offer_summary -> Offer.summary() -> get_offered_amounts() -> _get_offered_coins(). _get_offered_coins() guesses which created coins are 'offered' to the taker by matching addition amounts against the inner-puzzle CREATE_COIN(OFFER_MOD_HASH) amounts, with a fallback (offer.py:267-285) that, when the amount-match count is too low, resets to ALL additions and re-filters only by settlement puzzle hash. The code comments themselves call this 'a little heuristic' and 'imperfect for sufficiently complex spends'. The amounts the taker actually PAYS are the exact requested_payments (offer.py:314-321), but what the taker BELIEVES they will RECEIVE is the heuristic offered amount. A maker who can construct a spend bundle whose heuristic-estimated offered amount exceeds the coins the taker actually receives on-chain could induce the taker to accept an unfavorable trade. The on-chain settlement is still atomic and self-consistent (no coins are stolen beyond what the bundle encodes), so this is deception of the human decision, not a cryptographic bypass.
findings.exploitability: Low-to-moderate as social engineering, not a direct exploit. Reachability is via a victim choosing to view/take a counterparty offer (marketplace flow) or via the mTLS-gated RPC. I could not construct and run a concrete bundle proving the heuristic over-reports while on-chain delivery under-delivers (dependencies not installed), so confidence is low. The atomic announcement binding via OFFER_MOD prevents classic theft; the residual risk is purely that the displayed summary diverges from what the user nets.
findings.preconditions: Victim inspects/accepts a maker-crafted offer; maker must construct a spend bundle whose _get_offered_coins heuristic over-estimates the offered amount relative to coins actually delivered to the taker.
findings.code_excerpt: # This method does not get every coin that is being offered, only the `settlement_payment` children\n# It's also a little heuristic, but it should get most things\ndef _get_offered_coins(self) -> dict[bytes32 | None, list[Coin]]:

findings.title: get_cancellation_coins runs each coin spend with full INFINITE_COST budget (no shared cost cap) on attacker-influenced offer bytes
findings.source: Offer bytes stored from a taken counterparty offer (TradeRecord.offer), re-parsed in TradeManager.cancel_pending_offers via Offer.from_bytes(trade.offer).get_cancellation_coins() (trade_manager.py:271)
findings.sink: run_with_cost(spend.puzzle_reveal, INFINITE_COST, spend.solution) executed once per coin spend with a fresh 11e9 budget each (no cumulative decrement), plus an O(n^2)-ish dependency-elimination loop
findings.data_flow: Unlike __post_init__ (offer.py:165-177) and conditions() (offer.py:185-194), which decrement a single shared MAX_BLOCK_COST_CLVM/INFINITE_COST budget across all spends, get_cancellation_coins gives EACH spend a fresh INFINITE_COST (11e9) budget at offer.py:430 and additionally executes detect_dependent_coin in nested while-loops over coin_names x dependencies x announcements (offer.py:443-464). For an offer aggregated from many small coin spends (a 6 MiB decompressed bundle can hold a large number of spends; decompression is capped at 6 MiB but spend count is not separately bounded), total CLVM work scales with N x per-spend-cost and the elimination loop is roughly quadratic. cancel_pending_offers is reachable from the cancel_offer/cancel_offers wallet RPC; the offer content originates from a counterparty whose offer the victim previously took (stored as the aggregated trade.offer). The 6 MiB cap and 11e9 per-spend cost cap bound the worst case, so this is degraded responsiveness, not unbounded.
findings.exploitability: Low. Requires (1) the victim to have taken/stored a maker-crafted offer, then (2) the victim to cancel it, invoking get_cancellation_coins. mTLS gates the RPC. The cumulative work is bounded by the 6 MiB bundle size and the 11e9 per-spend cost ceiling, so it is slow rather than catastrophic. Also note opcode-60/61 parsing at offer.py:434-437 can raise (bytes32() on a non-32-byte as_python() result) producing an unhandled exception during cancellation.
findings.preconditions: Victim has stored a counterparty offer (took it) and later cancels it; maker packed many/expensive coin spends into the bundle.

result.coverage_notes:
Scope covered in full: chia/wallet/trading/offer.py (read entirely), trade_manager.py (entirely), trading/trade_store.py and trade_record.py, util/puzzle_compression.py (entirely), util/compute_hints.py, outer_puzzles.py, conditions.py (CreateCoin/announcement paths), nft_wallet.py make_nft1_offer + compute_royalty_amount, data_layer_wallet.py get_offer_summary/finish_graftroot_solutions, data_layer/data_layer_server.py + download_data.is_filename_valid, wallet_rpc_api.py offer endpoints, wallet_request_types.py offer request types, bech32m.py, rpc_server.py (auth + error handling).

Key negative results (things that looked promising but are adequately defended, with reasoning):
1) Decompression bomb: decompress_with_zdict caps zlib output at 6 MiB via decompress(blob, max_length=6*1024*1024); I empirically confirmed zlib leaves the remainder in unconsumed_tail and the cap holds, so no unbounded-memory bomb. Compression version is bounded (decompress_object_with_puzzles rejects version > len(ZDICT)=7), so zdict construction is bounded.
2) Fund theft via crafted offer: NOT reachable from the Python layer. Offer settlement integrity is enforced on-chain by OFFER_MOD (settlement_payments) via puzzle/coin announcements that atomically bind maker requested_payments to taker-provided coins; the wallet code only constructs spends, it does not arbitrate value. A lying driver_dict/royalty value causes the maker's on-chain spend to fail (whole bundle invalid) rather than transferring funds. compute_royalty_amount (nft_wallet.py:60-68) caps percentage at 10000 bps and royalty < offered amount, with overflow handled by uint64.
3) data_layer HTTP file server path traversal: is_filename_valid (download_data.py:28-59) parses the filename into fields and requires round-trip equality to the canonically regenerated name (reformatted == filename), which defeats ../ traversal. No traversal found.
4) Auth: wallet/full_node/data_layer RPC servers use mutual TLS with the node's private CA (rpc_server.py:177-205), so offer endpoints are NOT reachable by an unauthenticated remote P2P peer; the realistic adversary for the offer cluster is a counterparty whose offer file a victim chooses to process, or a holder of a valid client cert. This materially lowers severity versus the unauthenticated-P2P threat the prompt prioritizes.
5) Unhandled exceptions during offer parsing (uint64() on negative/oversized amounts in CreateCoin.from_program; bytes32() in get_cancellation_coins; StopIteration in DL get_offer_summary) are all caught by the RPC safe_handle wrapper (rpc_server.py:404) and converted to per-request errors, not process crashes.

Honest overall: zero high-confidence remote-unauthenticated findings in this cluster. The three reported items are low-severity robustness/deception issues whose reachability depends on mTLS-authenticated RPC access or a victim taking a counterparty offer. I did not have the chia_rs/chia_puzzles_py dependencies installed, so I could not execute PoCs; confidence is calibrated down accordingly, especially for the heuristic-summary deception (0.3) where I could not empirically demonstrate divergence between displayed and delivered amounts.

findings.title: Unbatched SQL IN-clause in get_generators_at can raise sqlite3.OperationalError on peer-supplied generator-ref lists
findings.source: UnfinishedBlock.transactions_generator_ref_list (list[uint32]) supplied by any peer via full_node_protocol.RespondUnfinishedBlock -> FullNodeAPI.respond_unfinished_block
findings.sink: BlockStore.get_generators_at: SELECT ... WHERE in_main_chain=1 AND height in ({'?,'*(len(heights)-1)}?) with one bound parameter per requested ref height (no batching, unlike get_block_records_by_hash which uses to_batches)
findings.data_flow: FullNodeAPI.respond_unfinished_block(respond_unfinished_block.unfinished_block, peer) -> FullNode.add_unfinished_block(block, peer): after validate_unfinished_block_header passes, line ~2528 `if block.transactions_generator is not None and len(block.transactions_generator_ref_list) > 0:` builds generator_refs = set(block.transactions_generator_ref_list) and calls Blockchain.lookup_block_generators(prev_header_hash, generator_refs). For main-chain prev, remaining_refs == generator_refs is passed to BlockStore.get_generators_at(remaining_refs), which formats one '?' placeholder per height. If the set size exceeds the SQLite host-parameter limit (~999 or 32766), conn.execute raises sqlite3.OperationalError('too many SQL variables'). The respond_unfinished_block rate limit (RLSettings(True,200,2*1024*1024)) allows a 2MB message, i.e. ~500k uint32 refs, far above the limit.
findings.preconditions: Attacker is any connected full-node peer (no auth). Node not in sync mode (respond_unfinished_block returns early if get_sync_mode()). The crafted UnfinishedBlock must pass validate_unfinished_block_header (proof-of-space/foliage header checks) and have prev_header_hash referencing a known block; transactions_generator must be present and ref-list length must exceed the SQLite variable limit.
findings.exploitability: Low. Even if reached, the exception is caught in WSChiaConnection._api_call (server/ws_connection.py:606) and only results in closing/banning the offending connection (API_EXCEPTION_BAN_SECONDS) — it does not crash the node or exhaust memory (the 2MB message cap bounds allocation, and SQLite rejects the statement before per-row work). The main practical barrier is constructing an UnfinishedBlock whose header validates while carrying an oversized ref list; consensus header validation likely rejects such blocks first, making real reachability uncertain.
findings.code_excerpt:
formatted_str = (
    f"SELECT block, height from full_blocks WHERE in_main_chain=1 AND height in ({'?,' * (len(heights) - 1)}?)"
)
... conn.execute(formatted_str, list(heights))  # not batched; one bind var per ref height

result.coverage_notes: Scope reviewed (all read with concrete data-flow from untrusted source to sink): chia/full_node/full_node_api.py (every @metadata.request handler, lines 177-2247), full_node.py (new_peak L784, _sync L1034, request_validate_wp L1137, sync_from_fork_point L1195, add_block_batch L1595, add_unfinished_block L2429, short_sync_backtrack/batch L614-777, compact-vdf paths L3292-3382), weight_proof.py (validate_weight_proof L605, validate_weight_proof_inner L1711, _validate_sub_epoch_summaries L848, _validate_sub_epoch_segments L950, sampling/segment-cap logic), subscriptions.py, coin_store.py (get_coin_states_by_* / batch_coin_states_by_puzzle_hashes), block_store.py (get_generators_at, get_block_records_by_hash). Cross-cutting mitigations examined: protocols/full_node_protocol.py + wallet_protocol.py (message field types), server/api_protocol.py (list_limits applied at parse via Streamable.from_bytes), util/streamable.py (parse_list / parse_list_limited), server/rate_limit_numbers.py (per-type frequency + max_size), server/ws_connection.py (_read_one_message 50MB WS cap, inbound rate limiting, _api_call L480 exception isolation).\n\nKey reasons no high/critical remote finding is reported: (1) Peer-supplied list lengths are truncated at PARSE time by list_limits for the subscription/state handlers (register_for_ph_updates -> max_subscriptions, register_for_coin_updates, request_puzzle_state -> MAX_PUZZLE_HASH_BATCH_SIZE, request_coin_state -> max_subscribe_response_items). (2) Range handlers are hard-capped: request_blocks/request_header_blocks by MAX_BLOCK_COUNT_PER_REQUESTS=32, request_block_headers by 128, request_additions/request_removals by MAX_COIN_HASHES_PER_REQUEST=50. (3) Per-message size (50MB WS cap; tighter per-type max_size) and per-minute frequency limits bound allocation/CPU per message. (4) Coin/puzzle DB queries all use LIMIT max_items and to_batches(SQLITE_MAX_VARIABLE_NUMBER). (5) Expensive handlers gate behind semaphores (wallet_sync_api_sem, new_peak_sem, compact_vdf_sem, sp_catchup_sem) and sync-mode/synced() checks. (6) Per-connection subscription limits cap memory per peer. (7) Crucially, unhandled handler exceptions (asserts, KeyError, ConsensusError, OperationalError) are caught in _api_call and only ban the offending connection — they do NOT crash the node, which neutralizes the unhandled-exception-crash class for the synchronous handler path. (8) Weight-proof validation (the largest peer-data structure) is gated by _validate_summaries_weight tying sub_epoch count/weight to the local recent chain, uses sampling caps (MAX_SAMPLES, _max_sub_epoch_segments), and offloads heavy VDF/recent-block work to a ProcessPoolExecutor. I could not construct a concrete, high-confidence path where a single untrusted peer message causes unbounded CPU/memory or a process-level crash. Background tasks spawned by handlers (tx_request_and_timeout, _sync, eventually_clear) handle their own exceptions internally. Remaining residual risk is aggregate/amplification load (e.g. many peers issuing rate-limited-but-DB-heavy request_block_headers or subscription queries), which is a design tradeoff rather than a discrete bug. The single reported item is a genuine unbatched-query robustness defect but is low impact due to exception isolation and message-size bounds; reported at Low/0.2 rather than overstated.

result.reason:
Data flow verified against real code and survives. subscribe RPC (data_layer_rpc_api.py:360-371, request['urls']) -> DataLayer.subscribe (data_layer.py:869-876) applies only url.rstrip('/'), stores ServerInfo, and the periodic loop (periodically_manage_data -> update_subscription -> fetch_and_validate, data_layer.py:1076-1085, 607-675) feeds server_info.url into http_download (download_data.py:298-318) which does session.get(server_info.url + '/' + filename). No scheme/host/IP/loopback/private-range validation exists at any hop -- confirmed. So the SSRF mechanism is real.

Two threat-model nuances refine the severity rather than refute it:
1) DIRECT RPC vector is operator-only. The DataLayer RPC server uses ssl_context_for_server with verify_mode=ssl.CERT_REQUIRED against the node's PRIVATE CA (server.py:67-72) and binds to self_hostname. An external party cannot call subscribe/add_mirror directly; doing so requires the node's own RPC client cert. Operator-pointing-own-node is self-SSRF (negligible).
2) The genuinely remote vector is the ON-CHAIN MIRROR path: update_subscriptions_from_wallet (data_layer.py:953-959) pulls mirror URLs via dl_get_mirrors -- those are mirror coins anyone can publish on-chain for any store_id via their own add_mirror. A victim node subscribed to that store will, in its periodic loop, fetch the attacker-supplied URL through the same unvalidated http_download. That is a remotely-triggerable, no-cert-required, blind GET SSRF against subscribed nodes.

Exploit is constrained, which caps it at Low: it is GET-only; blind (the response is streamed to a .dat file and validated as a delta tree, never reflected to the attacker); aiohttp restricts schemes to http/https (no file://), so no local file read; and the request path is fixed as url + '/' + get_delta_filename(...).dat, so the attacker cannot append arbitrary paths (e.g. cannot reach the AWS metadata path /latest/meta-data/...). Residual impact: forcing the node to issue outbound GETs to arbitrary internal hosts/ports (internal port scan / reachability oracle via timing/connection errors logged, hitting fire-on-GET internal endpoints) plus a MaxDeltaFileSizeExceeded/size sink. Real but low impact; candidate's Low rating is correct.
result.exploit_sketch: Remote (no RPC cert needed) variant: Attacker runs an internal-network-adjacent or on-chain actor. (1) Attacker creates a DataLayer mirror coin on-chain for a store_id that a victim node is subscribed to, using add_mirror with urls=["http://10.0.0.5:6379"] (or http://169.254.169.254 etc.). This is published via the wallet (DLNewMirror, data_layer.py:942-944) and costs only the mirror coin amount+fee. (2) On the victim node, periodically_manage_data -> update_subscription -> update_subscriptions_from_wallet (data_layer.py:1084, 953-959) reads that mirror URL from dl_get_mirrors and stores it as a server for the store. (3) fetch_and_validate (data_layer.py:624-675) selects the URL and download_file/http_download issues session.get("http://10.0.0.5:6379/<delta>.dat") from the victim host. Result: blind, GET-only SSRF -- victim node connects outbound to an attacker-chosen internal host/port. Useful as an internal reachability/port-scan oracle and to force connections to internal HTTP services that act on a GET to a fixed path. Not usable for cloud-metadata exfil (cannot control the appended path) and not for local file read (http/https only). Local/operator variant via the subscribe/add_mirror RPC directly is self-SSRF and negligible because the RPC requires the node's own CERT_REQUIRED client certificate.
